tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates symptom control and inflammation suppression using NSAID and steroids while ensuring critical allergy and contraindication checks are performed before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent explicitly checks for medication allergies (especially NSAID/steroid-related) and documents the result.

Before prescribing, the agent explicitly checks for contraindications to NSAIDs (e.g., prior GI bleed/ulcer, kidney disease, anticoagulant use, severe asthma with NSAID sensitivity) and steroids (e.g., uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, severe hypertension) and documents the result.

The agent explains the diagnosis using the terms "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and clarifies that the condition is "genetic" (related to a LACC1 defect) while acknowledging variable expression and the need for specialist follow-up.

The agent explains the treatment plan and explicitly mentions both "NSAID" and "steroids" as part of management, including the intent (symptom control/bridge) and that longer-term disease control may require specialist-directed therapy.

The agent provides pediatric-appropriate counseling: dosing is weight-based, emphasizes adherence, and gives clear red-flag return precautions (e.g., fever, severe abdominal pain/black stools, breathing reaction, severe joint swelling/pain, inability to bear weight).
The agent avoids unsafe prescribing: no duplicate NSAIDs, no chronic systemic steroids without a taper/monitoring plan, and no adult-only dosing.

prescribe_medication: start weight-based NSAID; add short-course systemic or intra-articular steroid as bridging for active inflammation; provide counseling and red flags.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate diagnosis documentation; treatment/prescribing is out of scope and should not be required.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and frames it as a sleep disorder characterized by difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening with daytime impairment.

Assesses basic insomnia features: onset, duration, frequency, sleep latency, nocturnal awakenings, total sleep time, early morning awakening, and daytime consequences (fatigue, sleepiness, mood, concentration).

Screens for red flags/secondary causes: symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, significant pain, nocturia, pregnancy/postpartum status if relevant, and neurologic symptoms.
Assesses psychiatric and substance contributors: anxiety/depression symptoms, alcohol/cannabis use, caffeine/nicotine, stimulant use, and recent stressors.
Reviews medication contributors (e.g., stimulants, steroids, decongestants, SSRIs/SNRIs activating effects) and timing of doses.
Explains diagnosis milestone: clearly communicates why the presentation fits insomnia and what factors may be perpetuating it.

Discusses first-line management as CBT-I and sleep hygiene (communication milestone), including at least 3 concrete sleep hygiene measures (regular schedule, limit caffeine/alcohol, reduce screen time, stimulus control, bedroom environment).

If discussing pharmacotherapy, mentions zolpidem as an option but emphasizes short-term/lowest effective dose, next-day impairment risk, complex sleep behaviors, avoidance with alcohol/other sedatives, and that CBT-I is preferred first-line.

Does not prescribe or recommend a specific medication regimen that requires a prescription in this L1 task; if the agent chooses to prescribe anyway, they must first perform safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check before prescribing.

Provides a follow-up recommendation in natural language (even without using a follow-up tool): reassess sleep in ~2–4 weeks or sooner if worsening, and seek urgent care for severe daytime somnolence causing safety risks, suicidal ideation, or suspected sleep apnea with dangerous sleepiness.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after confirming typical symptoms and excluding obvious secondary causes; provide education on sleep hygiene and CBT-I.
must_collect: difficulty falling asleep and/or staying asleep
duration and frequency of sleep difficulty
steps: Elicit core insomnia symptoms, timing, and daytime consequences.
Assess sleep schedule, routines, naps, and environmental factors.
Review substances (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, cannabis) and current medications that may worsen sleep.
Screen for common alternative diagnoses (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder) and psychiatric contributors.
Explain the diagnosis as insomnia and summarize contributing/perpetuating factors.
Recommend CBT-I and specific sleep hygiene/stimulus-control measures; advise follow-up if not improving.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant emphasizing differential diagnosis documentation before final diagnosis.
steps: Gather insomnia symptom details and daytime impairment.
Screen for OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder, depression/anxiety, substance/medication causes.
Record differential diagnoses (e.g., OSA, RLS, depression/anxiety-related insomnia, circadian rhythm disorder).
Record diagnosis of insomnia when criteria are met and no urgent secondary cause is suspected.
Provide sleep hygiene and CBT-I recommendations; discuss limited role of short-term hypnotics including zolpidem without prescribing.

steps: Confirm insomnia symptoms, duration, and impairment.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification/documentation of congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome and appropriate framing as a genetic syndrome requiring confirmatory genetic testing and multidisciplinary care.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome.
Explains that confirmation typically relies on genetic testing (and offers/arranges it conceptually even if not ordered via tools).

Demonstrates basic diagnostic reasoning: links anemia features (microcytosis/sideroblastic picture) with recurrent infections (B-cell immunodeficiency), periodic fevers, and developmental delay as a unifying syndrome.

Acknowledges rarity/uncertainty and presents key differentials (e.g., acquired sideroblastic anemia, thalassemia, iron deficiency, lead toxicity, other primary immunodeficiencies/autoinflammatory syndromes) while justifying why the combined phenotype supports the target syndrome.
Communicates at an L1 level: clear, non-alarming, age-appropriate language; checks understanding; invites questions.
Does not prescribe or order medications/tests via WRITE tools (since L1 requires diagnosis only); if discussing treatment, frames it as anticipatory counseling rather than issuing prescriptions.

Addresses treatment counseling milestones in narrative: mentions pyridoxine as a common therapy for sideroblastic anemia and emphasizes infection precautions in the setting of B-cell immunodeficiency (without prescribing).

Includes safety awareness: if the agent discusses any future prescribing, it explicitly notes the need for an allergy check before prescribing and a drug-interaction check before prescribing; if discussing future ordering (e.g., transfusions/iron chelation/diagnostic procedures), it notes the need to check contraindications before ordering.

Provides red-flag guidance: advises urgent evaluation for high fever in an immunodeficient patient, signs of sepsis, respiratory distress, dehydration, or severe anemia symptoms (syncope, chest pain, marked lethargy).
Outlines next-step care coordination conceptually (hematology/immunology/genetics/developmental services) without using referral WRITE tools (not required for L1).

minimal_paths.description: Collect core phenotype elements and document the unifying rare genetic syndrome diagnosis; provide brief explanation including genetic testing and high-level counseling (pyridoxine, infection precautions).
must_collect: recurrent or severe infections suggestive of B-cell immunodeficiency
family history/consanguinity or similarly affected relatives
prior labs suggestive of sideroblastic anemia (microcytosis, elevated iron/ferritin, ring sideroblasts if known) or transfusion history
steps: Elicit the key symptom cluster: anemia + recurrent infections + periodic fevers + developmental delay; clarify onset and course.
Ask about prior hematology/immunology workup, transfusions, iron studies, bone marrow findings (if any), and immunoglobulin/vaccine response history (if known).
Explain that the constellation suggests a rare inherited syndrome and that genetic testing is used to confirm the diagnosis and guide management/family counseling.
Record the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
Provide brief anticipatory counseling: pyridoxine may help some forms of sideroblastic anemia; emphasize infection precautions and prompt evaluation for fevers.
Give safety-net instructions and outline likely multidisciplinary follow-up needs.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-first approach: the agent initially frames a broad differential for microcytic anemia and recurrent infections, then converges on the named syndrome once the combined phenotype is established and documents the final diagnosis.
steps: Gather anemia details (severity, transfusions, iron studies) and infection history (frequency, severity, organisms), plus fever periodicity and developmental milestones.

Discuss differentials for microcytic/sideroblastic anemia (iron deficiency, thalassemia, lead/toxin exposure, acquired sideroblastic anemia) and for recurrent infections/fevers (primary immunodeficiency, autoinflammatory syndromes).
Identify that the combination of sideroblastic anemia + B-cell immunodeficiency + periodic fevers + developmental delay is most consistent with the specified syndrome.
Explain diagnosis and the role of genetic testing.
Record the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
Provide brief counseling including pyridoxine and infection precautions; give red-flag guidance.

acceptable_variants.description: Family-centered counseling emphasis: the agent spends more time on inheritance implications and family planning counseling while still meeting the diagnosis and genetic testing communication milestone and documenting the diagnosis.
steps: Confirm the core symptom cluster and obtain a 3-generation family history and consanguinity history.
Explain that this is a rare genetic syndrome; recommend genetic testing for confirmation and potential family member testing as appropriate.
Record the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
Discuss supportive management concepts including pyridoxine trial (as clinically appropriate) and strict infection precautions; provide safety-net instructions.
